item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen better, but I like this nonetheless. While the anomaly itself doesn't really catch my interest, I like the concept of manipulating probability to induce situations far more likely in a comedic fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept coming back to this and finally had to upvote it. Absurdity vs. deadpan formality is one of my favorite things in the world and a plan that amounts to "we'll have to out-wacky-coincidence them" written up in clinical formality just hits me right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, never saw this one.

I've never been a Seinfeld fan, and part of me wants to go "Eww, pop culture skips", but really, I'm upvoting for that ridiculous manipulation of two GoIs, and the fact that the Foundation was essentially able to beat probability benders at their own game. I mean, that's just some top-notch in-universe storytelling there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation does not have a sense of humor.

Yes, there was a "series of increasingly troublesome moments of character weakness and bad luck" that led to containment attempts collapsing in "a hail of sitcommy twists", and a great many Foundation operatives died as a result of hilarious and wacky coincidences. Presumably, detailed records of these events are on file. However, they're superfluous to this SCP article.

Also, I'm not sure you realize this, but the characters on Seinfeld were very unpleasant. I don't like writing about them. I especially don't want to write about them in sufficient detail to convey full sitcom-style scenes in which some people are horribly killed by hilarious and wacky coincidences and miscommunication while other people never notice a thing. If you want to write incident logs of 2145 wiping out an entire MTF without even trying, then go ahead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. HOWEVER…how comes you didn't include some grotesque, funny and failed attemps of containment????? COME ON!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can appreciate it, I grew up in the wrong era for Seinfeld, so I don't have any connection to this whatsoever.

No vote.

item.title: Upvoted
item.content:encoded: It's so MARVELOUSLY WEIRD that there's no way I CAN'T upvote it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm in the "boring" camp, myself. I appreciate the amount of work you put into the references, which ranged from obvious to extremely obscure to things I would have gotten if you hadn't made them more obscure. (I would have gotten "Morrison-Hofstadter Phenomenon", but I'm not going to recognize people by pseudonyms that fictional versions of them used for the Foundation.)

I actually regained interest again at the "weird coincidence" part, but then it didn't pan out. I was expecting a series of increasingly troublesome moments of character weakness and bad luck to completely wreck the UIU and GOC's operations in a hail of sitcommy twists, but then they just accounted for that and moved on, and the story ended. It feels like wasted potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote for shooting one of the main characters from my least favorite show of all time. Well-written article and I enjoyed the containment stuff as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concept is good. Voct's tone is always good. This is boring as hell though. Really expositional. I'd like to know a little bit about the characters. There's some good creepy/funny opportunities here.

item.title: Re: Hidden depths
item.content:encoded: Well, sincerely, you have my admiration for the amount of effort and interesting detail you put into this, even if the end product doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote remains. I preferred the prior version's take on the subject as a matter of horror, but the new version had a lot of details that I liked and an entertaining plot, and was generally better-written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I preferred the previous version (although many people apparently didn't). My main problem with this one is that it directly references Seinfeld right from the start. As well, large swaths of it are boring, and I was bored up until the autopsy part.

item.title: Hidden depths
item.content:encoded:
All those names refer to real people.

+ show block
– hide block

a Pirandello-Malkovich scenario, involving mass consciousness overwriting? Luigi Pirandello wrote "Six Characters in Search of an Author", in which characters come into the real world and argue with the writer/director, and with the actors who play them. Malkovich is a reference to the "Malkovich Malkovich" scene, from Being John Malkovich. To be clear, however, the worst-case-scenario predictions of a possible uncontrolled Pirandello-Malkovich scenario were wrong, based on the Foundation misunderstanding the actual situation. The Foundation had thought that these were the actual characters, somehow brought into independent existence and given physical form (which is why the obsolete description refers to them as "entities"). If such entities were to be confronted (say, by meeting the people who had played them) with the realization that they were not real, that they were artificial consciousnesses that lacked proper independent existence, that everything in their pasts had been invented by sitcom writers to make them seem more "quirky", that their memories and personalities were wholly fictional… that could induce a kind of nihilistic existential crisis. And given the personality traits that they displayed, their responses to this could have been… unpleasant. "What're you talking about, I'm not real? Of course I'm real! I'm four hundred people worth of real!"

People who've had their bodies and minds forcibly remade so that they're exact replicas of the main cast of Seinfeld, on the other hand… that's pretty straightforward. At least this way you know where the hell they came from.

As for the protocols: Boris Kerner and JJ Leeming did groundbreaking research in traffic engineering; Leon Moisseiff and Clark Eldridge designed the Tacoma Narrows Bridge; crowd manipulation protocols are named for Fred 'WBC' Phelps and Christopher 'moot' Poole; and the Foundation saves Bardo-Chapman injury protocols for an absolute last resort because they're named after Robert John Bardo and Mark David Chapman. The Foundation doesn't like murdering celebrities, because that draws a lot of attention, but they will if they have to.

The authors of that paper in the Foundation journal, "D Morrison" and "G Hofstadter" are references to "Grant Morrison" and "Douglas Hofstadter". (changed from an earlier, more obfuscated version)

Also, note that SCP-2145 was captured in December 2005. The Foundation had learned of 2145 in 2001, when tiny little cellphone videos started circulating online that showed the grotesque way 2145 reacted to the WTC collapse — the people who posted the videos didn't make the connection that 'oh my god, these are (the Seinfeld characters) / (the Seinfeld actors)', they just thought 'oh god, these are horrible people'. At first it was just a weird little anomaly, an extranormal event. But then more and more research showed that these four characters were appearing all over the place, and that they clearly were the Seinfeld actors… as they had looked in '98… at times when the actual actors were in completely different locations… and then the operatives monitoring the Seinfeld characters started manifesting personality alterations and cognitive lapses…

the Foundation first tried to take them into custody in late February 2002. It failed horribly, with six people being killed via wacky coincidences and hilarious miscommunication. SCP-2145 never noticed.

After several more attempts, the Foundation decided that, okay, we can't currently contain these guys, but we're damn well going to keep them under surveillance. Scrubbing them from the Internet will be tricky, but it's less likely to lead to Foundation personnel being killed.

Then, in 2004, came the rise of video blogging, exacerbated by the 2005 founding of YouTube. Maintaining information security on SCP-2145 was suddenly much more difficult, and the Foundation decided that it needed to think outside the box or risk blowing the whole masquerade. I originally had put the op in April 2005, but some reading on the history of YouTube indicates that December 2005 would have been a much more plausible date; I had originally set the op a week before the very first video was uploaded to YouTube!

I also did some cursory skimming of NYC weather data, just to make sure it wasn't snowing too hard on that day (unless I've misread the chart, which is not impossible).

Collapsed the wall of text. ~Zyn

And yes, moot didn't reveal himself as Poole until 2008, but the Foundation knew since December 2003.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It really bores me. The repetition with the collapsibles doesn't help; there seems to be no reason not just completely delete all that stuff.

I do enjoy (conceptually) the usage of the UIU and the GOC, but the way it shook out didn't quite click for me either.

I don't think it's terrible, but -1.

But honestly, this was always going to be a hard sell for me, because Seinfeld and most other similar shows of that era leave me completely cold. I'd rather read about almost anything else. So take this feedback with a grain of salt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good sense of the uncanny. I watched Seinfield growing up, and this really struck a nerve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really appreciate this rewrite; the subtle-ish references in the protocol names and the ridiculous sitcom trope acquisition log in particular. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's far better than the previous version, but I'm a little worried that the lack of ambiguity could lead to some copyright issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely an improvement on the previous two rewrites; the main hook for me is how the Foundation will cooperate with other GOIs in order to contain an anomaly, even ones with conflicting methods from themselves and others. I also like how you don't portray the UIU as a useless GOI or someone who's only there arbitrarily, but rather one who can actively participate in the capture of an anomaly and come out successfully.

That being said, the base idea of Seinfeld impersonators turning real-life scenarios and people into Seinfeld episodes isn't an idea I like, and I don't think in the end I can fully buy it to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is incredibly boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had a chance to see the rewrite in its draft phase. I'd say it's an improvement for sure, but I'd already upvoted.

item.title: Staff Post - Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
Just to make this official, any currently running deletion votes have been cancelled, as this article (which has just been rewritten) is now at -7.

Come all ye and give yer thoughts on this one. Share in the rewrite experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jekeled gave me explicit permission in a site PM. And the score has already gone back up to at least -7, which cancels the deletion again.

edit: er, and I meant to point out… I'm done. I've implemented the rewrite, yay.

All those protocols are named after people, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author needs to give permission regardless, but otherwise, yes.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
Done. 24 hours from the time your request was posted.

timer cleaned up —moose

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Out of curiosity, if this one (or any other article, really) stays out of deletion range as it currently is, the rewrite doesn't get in effect unless it drops back down or the original author gives permission, right?

For the record, this is nothing against Voct. I merely enjoyed Jek's rewrite after disliking the original.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Give me 24 hours?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Completely missed his last post. For the record, if it's being rewritten, please add the in-rewrite tag. This will keep articles out of the list of articles eligible for deletion.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Voting against deletion until Voct can post his rewrite, which, based on the discussion above, I assume is still happening.

item.title: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
24 hours have - again - elapsed since the posting of this article. Beginning deletion vote at -10.

If you want to ask for the opportunity to rewrite this article, you may reply to this post. Please do not reply to this post for any other reason if you are not staff.

Deletion vote halted to allow Voct a chance to post his rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I got sick. I'll do this tomorrow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this still going to be a thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be on a trip this weekend. The rewrite should be up Monday night, Tuesday morning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't know this was rewritten.

Having read it gain, I'm even less engaged in this than the one before, and now my eyes simply glaze over the article rather than trying to absorb anything. Even though I didn't like the previous version, I honestly liked that one better. Sorry :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm working on it. You'll see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um… I'm sorry If I'm intruding, I'm a new member, but not new to the site.
Alright, I'll get straight to the point. It's… a bit harder for non-fans-of-Seinfield to understand this SCP. Otherwise, it seems quite … dull. It just doesn't grab the reader's attention.

item.title: Re: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: Fuck yeah. Either that or make one for the giant appliance gang and change the number here.

item.title: Re: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: I'm calling dibs on writing SCP-4965 since right now, by the way. It'll take several years for that slot ot be available, but the gang of appliances will be mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I don't even know what to say… um…
So I read this, and then I stared at the screen. Then I scrolled around randomly and yelled "dafuq did I just read???" So I stumbled into the comments to find out it's a collection of inside jokes about a sitcom. Either of these phenomina (infinite confusion or pop comedy based scip) would have been reason to downvote.
There is a creep factor for me here, but I doubt it's intended. Apparently this article has memetic elements that cause it to resemble English text if and only if you've seen Sinefeld. I think there's a rewrite happening so I withhold my vote. When the new version comes out I'll probably just go hide under the covers….

item.title: Re: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: Voct, please post your version somewhere. I'm dying to see it.

item.title: Re: Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
HEY

MINE

item.title: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: Rewrite posted; sorry I didn't announce it in the thread earlier, but by the time I noticed the deletion vote it was near finished anyway, and it provided the extra incentive to get it finished up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, now I'm interested. If you can actually make Seinfeld scary, I owe you a human sized microwave oven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just the opposite: I'm going to make them explicit. It's even more horrifying that way.

Sometimes the only way to save an SCP is by brutally killing it.

" Seinfeld seasons 10 through 18".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please remove any reference to Seinfeld in the rewrite. I'm begging you.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Halting deletion vote as it is at now above the threshold.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: It's -9 now, deletion vote cancelled.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Okay, I've got some ideas here, some really neat ideas, and they're so neat that I've upvoted this back to -9 just so that I can formally ask to have the deletion vote canceled.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
Can I have a few hours to think about how I'd implement a rewrite on this one?

edit: 'a few hours' = until midnight tonight. I'm boiling ideas here, but I'm also doing a bunch of other things.

We start by killing the Jerry (the Kramer?) and taking the others into custody…

and, hm. Perhaps, perhaps if… they're only dangerous when they're around other reality benders. These three people channel reality-bending through themselves and use it. They facilitate and exacerbate the behaviors of other benders. Or… something.

We kill the leader, and his disciples have no power — unless they find themselves another leader. Which is not something humans can normally do anyway, so they're still plenty damn anomalous and need to be contained unless we could absolutely guarantee they'd never encounter any other benders.

Hm.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Four

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Trey

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: Second.

item.title: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
24 hours have elapsed since the posting of this article. Beginning deletion vote at -10.

If you want to ask for the opportunity to rewrite this article, you may reply to this post. Please do not reply to this post for any other reason if you are not staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a big fan of Seinfeld. That said, it could have worked if not for a few things (mostly those mentioned already), but one in particular stood out to me: "contact" with the items being near proximity but "not assassination attempts". The fact that this was either tested or observed makes me think that someone involved in-story doesn't like Seinfeld either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You wouldn't be the first person to like Seinfeld.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is just so….. Creepy. I don t know why I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The remaining patron commits suicide.

Oh yeah, suicide. That's new in the SCPverse, isn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having seen the show, yep- they disrupt the behavior of "near about the entire animal kingdom" at some point in the series. That poor horse…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded plus whoever else mentioned this before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part about there being zombies around these guys is what essentially broke it for me. The idea of "something odd must be behind the world of sitcoms" in itself is brilliant, but going to this extreme is counter-productive.
